claim: Use 4 spaces per indentation level and spaces only; tabs exist solely to stay consistent with already-tab-indented code, and Python refuses mixed indentation.

Keep lines within the project's limit (PEP 8 baseline: 79 chars code, 72 for docstrings/comments) and wrap long lines using implied continuation inside parentheses/brackets, preferring that over backslashes.
When wrapping expressions, break BEFORE binary operators (Knuth style) so each operator sits next to its operand: 'income = (gross_wages\n          + taxable_interest)'.
Surround top-level function and class definitions with two blank lines, methods inside a class with one, and use sparse blank lines inside functions only to mark logical sections.
With a hanging indent, put no arguments on the first line and indent continuation lines an extra level so they can't be confused with the nested block below.

key: General|Python|Imports|One import per line at the top
claim: Put imports on separate lines at the top of the file (after the module docstring, before globals); 'from x import a, b' on one line is fine, 'import sys, os' is not.
Group imports in three blocks separated by blank lines: standard library, third-party, then local application imports.
Prefer absolute imports for readability and better error behavior; explicit relative imports are acceptable in deep package layouts, but never implicit ones.
Avoid 'from module import *' — it hides which names exist in the namespace from both readers and tools; the one defensible use is republishing an internal interface as a public API.

key: General|Python|Strings|Pick one quote style be consistent
claim: Single and double quotes are equivalent: pick one and stick to it, switch to the other to avoid backslash-escaping, and always use double quotes for triple-quoted strings per docstring convention.
No spaces immediately inside parentheses/brackets/braces, before commas or colons, or before a call's or index's opening bracket: spam(ham[1], {eggs: 2}), never spam( ham[ 1 ] ).

Surround assignment, comparison, and boolean operators with exactly one space per side; optionally drop spaces around HIGHER-priority operators (x*x + y*y), never the reverse, and never align assignments with extra spaces.
No spaces around '=' for keyword arguments and unannotated defaults — complex(real, imag=0.0) — but DO space it when the parameter carries an annotation: def munge(sep: AnyStr = None).
Avoid compound statements: no 'if cond: do_thing()' on one line, never multiple clauses (if/else, try/finally) folded onto single lines, and no semicolon-chained statements.

key: General|Python|Layout|Trailing commas one value per line
claim: A one-element tuple gets parentheses and a trailing comma — FILES = ('setup.cfg',) — and lists expected to grow get one value per line with trailing commas so diffs stay one-line.

key: General|Python|Comments|Outdated comments are worse than none
claim: Comments that contradict the code are worse than no comments — updating comments alongside code changes is a priority, not a nicety.
Use inline comments sparingly, separated by two spaces, and never to restate the obvious: 'x = x + 1  # Compensate for border' is useful; '# Increment x' is noise.

Write docstrings for all public modules, functions, classes, and methods; non-public functions instead get a comment after the def line, and a multiline docstring's closing quotes sit on their own line.

key: General|Python|Naming|CapWords classes snake functions UPPER constants
claim: Classes use CapWords, functions/variables/methods use lower_snake_case, constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE at module level, and modules/packages get short all-lowercase names.

key: General|Python|Naming|Never l O I as single-char names
claim: Never use lowercase 'l', uppercase 'O', or uppercase 'I' as single-character names — in many fonts they're indistinguishable from 1 and 0.
One leading underscore marks internal/non-public names; reserve double-leading-underscore name mangling for avoiding attribute collisions in classes designed for subclassing, nothing else.
Exceptions are classes (CapWords) and carry the 'Error' suffix when they represent errors; signaling exceptions used for flow control take no suffix.
Instance methods take 'self' first, class methods take 'cls', and a name clashing with a keyword gets a trailing underscore (class_) instead of an abbreviation or misspelling.

Modules should declare their public names with __all__ (empty list = no public API), and even then keep internal names underscore-prefixed; imported names are implementation details unless explicitly re-exported.

key: General|Python|Naming|Public versus subclass API is a decision
claim: Decide explicitly whether each attribute is public, subclass-API, or internal — and when in doubt choose non-public, because making it public later is easy and the reverse is a breaking change.
Compare to singletons like None with 'is' / 'is not', never ==; and beware 'if x' when you mean 'if x is not None' — a falsy container value would take the wrong branch.
Build strings from pieces with ''.join(parts), not repeated '+=' in a loop — in-place concatenation is a fragile CPython-only optimization that other implementations don't have.
Use a def statement instead of binding a lambda to a name — the function gets a real name in tracebacks, and assignment removes the only advantage a lambda had.
Check prefixes and suffixes with .startswith() and .endswith(), not slice comparisons like foo[:3] == 'bar' — cleaner and immune to off-by-one length mistakes.
Test types with isinstance(obj, int), never 'type(obj) is type(1)' — isinstance respects inheritance and reads as intent.
Test emptiness with the sequence's own truthiness — 'if not seq:' — rather than 'if len(seq) == 0', and never compare booleans with '== True'.
User exceptions derive from Exception; BaseException is reserved for things that should almost never be caught (SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt).
Design exception hierarchies around the distinctions CATCHING code needs — answer 'what went wrong?' programmatically — not around where the exceptions happen to be raised.

key: General|Python|Exceptions|Bare except has two legal uses
claim: Catch named exceptions; a bare 'except:' also swallows SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt, so it's legitimate only when logging the traceback or doing cleanup before re-raising — and 'except Exception:' is the broad form that doesn't break Ctrl-C.
Limit the try clause to the minimum code that can raise the exception you're handling — wrapping extra calls inside the try masks their unrelated bugs as the handled case.
Use 'raise NewError(...) from original' for explicit replacement that keeps the original traceback; with 'from None', carry the inner exception's relevant details into the new message.
Don't use return/break/continue inside a finally block where it would jump out of it — doing so silently cancels any exception propagating through.

Use a with statement (or try/finally) for resources local to a code section so cleanup is prompt and guaranteed; invoke context managers through NAMED methods (conn.begin_transaction()) when they do more than acquire/release.
Either every return in a function returns an expression or none do — and where any does, bare exits must say 'return None' explicitly, including a reachable final return.

key: General|Python|Classes|All six rich comparisons or total_ordering
claim: When implementing ordering, implement all six comparisons (__eq__ through __ge__) or use functools.total_ordering — relying on the interpreter's argument swapping confuses other contexts.

Expose simple public data as plain attributes, not getter/setter methods — Python's properties let you add behavior later without breaking callers; keep property access cheap and side-effect-free because it LOOKS cheap.
Function annotations use PEP 484 syntax: spaces around the '->' arrow, a space after (never before) the colon — def munge(input: AnyStr) -> PosInt.

key: General|Python|Compatibility|Style yields to consistency and compat
claim: Know when to be inconsistent: never break backwards compatibility just to satisfy a style rule, match surrounding code's existing style, and prefer readability over rule-following when they conflict — consistency within a module outranks consistency with the guide.
Source files are UTF-8 with no encoding declaration; identifiers stay ASCII and English wherever feasible, with non-ASCII characters reserved for data like human names and places.
